question:
3. Develop necessary and sufficient conditions that the equation
\[
\left|\begin{array}{ccc}
0 & a_{1}-x & a_{2}-x \\
-a_{1}-x & 0 & a_{3}-x \\
-a_{2}-x & -a_{3}-x & 0
\end{array}\right|=0 \quad\left(a_{i} \neq 0\right)
\]
shall have a multiple root.
solution:
Solution. The given determinant is
\[
-2 x^{3}+2\left(a_{1} a_{2}+a_{2} a_{3}-a_{1} a_{3}\right) x=0 .
\]

The necessary and sufficient condition for a multiple root is
\[
a_{1} a_{2}+a_{2} a_{3}-a_{1} a_{3}=0
\]

If \( a_{1} a_{2} a_{3} \neq 0 \), this condition can be expressed in the form
\[
\frac{1}{a_{1}}+\frac{1}{a_{3}}=\frac{1}{a_{2}} .
\]

Comment. We might consider a slightly more general problem. Let \( A \) be a \( 3 \times 3 \) skew-symmetric matrix and \( S \) a \( 3 \times 3 \) symmetric matrix. Let \( f(x)=\operatorname{det}(A-x S) \). Then
\[
\begin{aligned}
f(-x) & =\operatorname{det}(A+x S)=\operatorname{det}\left(A^{T}+x S^{T}\right) \\
& =\operatorname{det}(-A+x S)=-\operatorname{det}(A-x S)=-f(x) .
\end{aligned}
\]

So \( f \) must be an odd function, \( f(x)=\alpha x^{3}+\beta x \). A multiple root exists if abd only if \( \beta=0 \) and \( \alpha \neq 0 \).

descriptive_long.question:
3. Develop necessary and sufficient conditions that the equation
\[
\left|\begin{array}{ccc}
0 & firstparam-unknownvar & secondparam-unknownvar \\
-firstparam-unknownvar & 0 & thirdparam-unknownvar \\
-secondparam-unknownvar & -thirdparam-unknownvar & 0
\end{array}\right|=0 \quad\left(iterparam \neq 0\right)
\]
shall have a multiple root.
descriptive_long.solution:
Solution. The given determinant is
\[
-2\, unknownvar^{3}+2\left(firstparam\, secondparam+secondparam\, thirdparam-firstparam\, thirdparam\right) unknownvar=0 .
\]

The necessary and sufficient condition for a multiple root is
\[
firstparam\, secondparam+secondparam\, thirdparam-firstparam\, thirdparam=0
\]

If \( firstparam\, secondparam\, thirdparam \neq 0 \), this condition can be expressed in the form
\[
\frac{1}{firstparam}+\frac{1}{thirdparam}=\frac{1}{secondparam} .
\]

Comment. We might consider a slightly more general problem. Let \( skewmatrix \) be a \( 3 \times 3 \) skew-symmetric matrix and \( symmatrix \) a \( 3 \times 3 \) symmetric matrix. Let \( detfunction(unknownvar)=\operatorname{det}(skewmatrix-unknownvar\, symmatrix) \). Then
\[
\begin{aligned}
detfunction(-unknownvar) & =\operatorname{det}(skewmatrix+unknownvar\, symmatrix)=\operatorname{det}\left(skewmatrix^{T}+unknownvar\, symmatrix^{T}\right) \\
& =\operatorname{det}(-skewmatrix+unknownvar\, symmatrix)=-\operatorname{det}(skewmatrix-unknownvar\, symmatrix)=-detfunction(unknownvar) .
\end{aligned}
\]

So \( detfunction \) must be an odd function, \( detfunction(unknownvar)=cubecoeff\, unknownvar^{3}+linecoeff\, unknownvar \). A multiple root exists if and only if \( linecoeff=0 \) and \( cubecoeff \neq 0 \).

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
3. Develop necessary and sufficient conditions that the equation
\[
\left|\begin{array}{ccc}
0 & raspberry-dragonfly & blueberry-dragonfly \\
-raspberry-dragonfly & 0 & blackberry-dragonfly \\
-blueberry-dragonfly & -blackberry-dragonfly & 0
\end{array}\right|=0 \quad\left(elderberry \neq 0\right)
\]
shall have a multiple root.
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
Solution. The given determinant is
\[
-2 dragonfly^{3}+2\left(raspberry\,blueberry+blueberry\,blackberry-raspberry\,blackberry\right) dragonfly=0 .
\]

The necessary and sufficient condition for a multiple root is
\[
raspberry\,blueberry+blueberry\,blackberry-raspberry\,blackberry=0
\]

If \( raspberry\,blueberry\,blackberry \neq 0 \), this condition can be expressed in the form
\[
\frac{1}{raspberry}+\frac{1}{blackberry}=\frac{1}{blueberry} .
\]

Comment. We might consider a slightly more general problem. Let \( pineapple \) be a \( 3 \times 3 \) skew-symmetric matrix and \( watermelon \) a \( 3 \times 3 \) symmetric matrix. Let \( gooseberry(dragonfly)=\operatorname{det}(pineapple-dragonfly\,watermelon) \). Then
\[
\begin{aligned}
gooseberry(-dragonfly) & =\operatorname{det}(pineapple+dragonfly\,watermelon)=\operatorname{det}\left(pineapple^{T}+dragonfly\,watermelon^{T}\right) \\
& =\operatorname{det}(-pineapple+dragonfly\,watermelon)=-\operatorname{det}(pineapple-dragonfly\,watermelon)=-gooseberry(dragonfly) .
\end{aligned}
\]

So \( gooseberry \) must be an odd function, \( gooseberry(dragonfly)=pomegranate\,dragonfly^{3}+boysenberry\,dragonfly \). A multiple root exists if abd only if \( boysenberry=0 \) and \( pomegranate \neq 0 \).

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
3. Develop necessary and sufficient conditions that the equation
\[
\left|\begin{array}{ccc}
0 & variableone-knownvalue & variabletwo-knownvalue \\
-variableone-knownvalue & 0 & variablethree-knownvalue \\
-variabletwo-knownvalue & -variablethree-knownvalue & 0
\end{array}\right|=0 \quad\left(variableindex \neq 0\right)
\]
shall have a multiple root.
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
Solution. The given determinant is
\[
-2 knownvalue^{3}+2\left(variableone variabletwo+variabletwo variablethree-variableone variablethree\right) knownvalue=0 .
\]

The necessary and sufficient condition for a multiple root is
\[
variableone variabletwo+variabletwo variablethree-variableone variablethree=0
\]

If \( variableone variabletwo variablethree \neq 0 \), this condition can be expressed in the form
\[
\frac{1}{variableone}+\frac{1}{variablethree}=\frac{1}{variabletwo} .
\]

Comment. We might consider a slightly more general problem. Let \( nonsymmetric \) be a \( 3 \times 3 \) skew-symmetric matrix and \( antisymmetric \) a \( 3 \times 3 \) symmetric matrix. Let \( constant(knownvalue)=\operatorname{det}(nonsymmetric-knownvalue antisymmetric) \). Then
\[
\begin{aligned}
constant(-knownvalue) & =\operatorname{det}(nonsymmetric+knownvalue antisymmetric)=\operatorname{det}\left(nonsymmetric^{T}+knownvalue antisymmetric^{T}\right) \\
& =\operatorname{det}(-nonsymmetric+knownvalue antisymmetric)=-\operatorname{det}(nonsymmetric-knownvalue antisymmetric)=-constant(knownvalue) .
\end{aligned}
\]

So \( constant \) must be an odd function, \( constant(knownvalue)=nonfactor knownvalue^{3}+nonparameter knownvalue \). A multiple root exists if abd only if \( nonparameter=0 \) and \( nonfactor \neq 0 \).

garbled_string.question:
3. Develop necessary and sufficient conditions that the equation
\[
\left|\begin{array}{ccc}
0 & qzxwvtnp-pldkjmas & hjgrksla-pldkjmas \\
-qzxwvtnp-pldkjmas & 0 & bqtmnsdf-pldkjmas \\
-hjgrksla-pldkjmas & -bqtmnsdf-pldkjmas & 0
\end{array}\right|=0 \quad\left(lyptzsnc \neq 0\right)
\]
shall have a multiple root.
garbled_string.solution:
Solution. The given determinant is
\[
-2\,pldkjmas^{3}+2\left(qzxwvtnp hjgrksla+hjgrksla bqtmnsdf-qzxwvtnp bqtmnsdf\right)pldkjmas=0 .
\]

The necessary and sufficient condition for a multiple root is
\[
qzxwvtnp hjgrksla+hjgrksla bqtmnsdf-qzxwvtnp bqtmnsdf=0
\]

If \( qzxwvtnp hjgrksla bqtmnsdf \neq 0 \), this condition can be expressed in the form
\[
\frac{1}{qzxwvtnp}+\frac{1}{bqtmnsdf}=\frac{1}{hjgrksla} .
\]

Comment. We might consider a slightly more general problem. Let \( ugotrhps \) be a \( 3 \times 3 \) skew-symmetric matrix and \( mnlkjhgf \) a \( 3 \times 3 \) symmetric matrix. Let \( rtyuiopa(pldkjmas)=\operatorname{det}(ugotrhps-pldkjmas mnlkjhgf) \). Then
\[
\begin{aligned}
rtyuiopa(-pldkjmas) & =\operatorname{det}(ugotrhps+pldkjmas mnlkjhgf)=\operatorname{det}\left(ugotrhps^{T}+pldkjmas mnlkjhgf^{T}\right) \\
& =\operatorname{det}(-ugotrhps+pldkjmas mnlkjhgf)=-\operatorname{det}(ugotrhps-pldkjmas mnlkjhgf)=-rtyuiopa(pldkjmas) .
\end{aligned}
\]

So \( rtyuiopa \) must be an odd function, \( rtyuiopa(pldkjmas)=asdfghjk\,pldkjmas^{3}+zxcvbnml\,pldkjmas \). A multiple root exists if and only if \( zxcvbnml=0 \) and \( asdfghjk \neq 0 \).

kernel_variant.question:
Let  

             0    c_1   c_2   c_3   
      K =  -c_1   0    c_4   c_5  ,    (c_1,\ldots ,c_6 \in  \mathbb{R}, not all zero),  
           -c_2 -c_4   0    c_6   
           -c_3 -c_5 -c_6   0    

be an arbitrary real 4 \times  4 skew-symmetric matrix.  
For every real parameter x put  

          M(x) = K - x I_4 ,  I_4 = 4 \times  4 identity.

Define the quartic polynomial  

          f(x) := det M(x).  (\star )

(a)  Show that f is an even polynomial of degree 4 and therefore can be written in the bi-quadratic form  

          f(x) = x^4 + \sigma  x^2 + \pi ,                               (1)

     and express the two scalar invariants \sigma  and \pi  solely in terms of the six entries c_1,\ldots ,c_6.

(b)  Give a necessary and sufficient condition, written only with c_1,\ldots ,c_6, for the quartic (\star ) to possess at least one multiple (possibly complex) root.

(c)  Under that condition classify the multiplicities of the roots and write down explicitly every repeated root, distinguishing the three possibilities  

     * x = 0 is a repeated root,  
     * x = \pm  i\sqrt{\sigma  / 2} are repeated roots,  
     * all four roots coincide.  

     Prove in particular that the last alternative can occur only when K = 0 and is therefore ruled out by the standing assumption ``not all c_i are zero''.

(The problem is the 4 \times  4 analogue of the classical 3 \times  3 cubic that involves the Pfaffian and tr K^2.)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout we write  

          S := c_1^2 + c_2^2 + c_3^2 + c_4^2 + c_5^2 + c_6^2 \geq  0,   
          P := c_1c_6 - c_2c_5 + c_3c_4  (the Pfaffian of K).

The Pfaffian is real, so P^2 \geq  0.

Step 1.  Reduction to the bi-quadratic form.  
For every 4 \times  4 skew matrix K one has the well-known identity

          det(\lambda  I_4 - K) = \lambda ^4 - \frac{1}{2} tr K^2 \lambda ^2 + pf(K)^2.          (2)

(An elementary derivation uses the block form of K and the Cayley-Hamilton
theorem.)

Replacing \lambda  by x and changing the overall sign (|K - xI| = | xI - K|) gives   

          f(x)=det(K - xI_4)=x^4 - \frac{1}{2} tr K^2 x^2 + pf(K)^2.         (3)

Hence  

          f(x)=x^4+\sigma x^2+\pi                                         (1)

with the invariants  

          \sigma  := -\frac{1}{2} tr K^2,  \pi  := pf(K)^2.                       (4)

Because K is skew, tr K = 0, hence f is even.  
Note that \sigma  \geq  0 and \pi  \geq  0.

Step 2.  \sigma  and \pi  in terms of c_1,\ldots ,c_6.  

2.1  The trace term.  
For a skew matrix, (K^2)_{ii}=\Sigma _j K_{ij}K_{ji}=-\Sigma _j K_{ij}^2.
Therefore  

          tr K^2 = -2 S, so \sigma  = -\frac{1}{2}(-2S) = S.                   (5)

Thus \sigma  is simply the sum of the squares of the six independent entries and
vanishes only when K = 0.

2.2  The Pfaffian.  
A direct expansion gives  

          pf(K)= P = c_1c_6 - c_2c_5 + c_3c_4,                       (6)
          \pi  = P^2.                                              (7)

Step 3.  Necessary and sufficient condition for a multiple root.  

With f(x)=x^4+\sigma x^2+\pi  we have  

          f'(x)=4x^3+2\sigma x = 2x(2x^2+\sigma ).                           (8)

A number x_0 is a multiple root of f  \Leftrightarrow   f(x_0)=0 and f'(x_0)=0.

(i)  x_0 = 0.  Then f'(0)=0 automatically, and 0 is a root \Leftrightarrow  \pi =0.   (9)

(ii) x_0 \neq  0.  Condition f'(x_0)=0 forces 2x_0^2+\sigma =0 \Leftrightarrow  x_0^2=-\sigma /2.
Inserting into f(x_0)=0 yields

        (-\sigma /2)^2 + \sigma (-\sigma /2) + \pi  = \pi  - \sigma ^2/4 = 0.                  (10)

Hence there exists a non-zero multiple root (necessarily imaginary unless \sigma =0)  

          \Leftrightarrow   \sigma ^2 = 4\pi  and \sigma  \neq  0.                               (11)

Combining (9) and (11):

          f has a multiple root \Leftrightarrow   \pi  = 0   or   \sigma ^2 = 4\pi .       (12)

Step 4.  Translation into the entries of K.  
Using (5) and (7),

          \sigma  = S, \pi  = P^2, \sigma ^2 = S^2.  

Condition (12) becomes  

   P\cdot (4P^2 - S^2) = 0.                                          (13)

Equivalently  

   (I)  P = 0, or (II)  S^2 = 4P^2.                              (14)

Because S and |P| are rotation-invariant quantities, (14) is a concise
entry-wise description.

Step 5.  Classification of multiplicities.  

Recall \sigma  = S \geq  0.

* Case (I) only (P = 0, S > 0).  
  Then \pi  = 0, so  

          f(x)=x^2(x^2+S).                                       (15)

  The root x = 0 has multiplicity 2, whereas  

          x = \pm  i\sqrt{S}                                            (16)

  are distinct and simple purely imaginary roots.

* Case (II) only (P \neq  0, S^2=4P^2, hence S>0).  
  Write \sigma =S and set \rho ^2 := S/2>0.  Then \pi  = \sigma ^2/4 = \rho ^4 and  

          f(x)=x^4+\sigma x^2+\sigma ^2/4 = (x^2+\rho ^2)^2.                         (17)

  The two purely imaginary numbers  

          x = \pm  i\rho  = \pm  i\sqrt{\sigma /2}                                 (18)

  are double roots.

* Both (I) and (II) (S=0=\sigma , P=0=\pi ).  
  Here f(x)=x^4, so x=0 would be a quadruple root.  
  However S=0 forces c_1=\ldots =c_6=0 by (5), contradicting the hypothesis
  ``not all c_i are zero''.  Hence this scenario cannot actually occur.

Consequently, for any non-zero 4 \times  4 skew matrix K the only possible
multiplicity patterns are:

   - a double root at 0 and two simple purely imaginary roots (Pfaffian 0);  
   - two conjugate double purely imaginary roots (\sigma ^2 = 4\pi  \neq  0).

--------------------------------------------------------------------

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
1. Higher dimension: The problem moves from a 3 × 3 to a 4 × 4 matrix, raising the characteristic polynomial from cubic to quartic.  
2. Additional mathematical structure: The solution exploits deep properties of skew–symmetric matrices—Pfaffians, trace identities and the even-degree nature of the characteristic polynomial.  
3. Deeper algebra: Detecting repeated roots now requires manipulating quartic discriminants and interpreting them through invariants σ, π, demanding facility with resultants and discriminants rather than the linear observation used in the original cubic.  
4. Multiple interacting concepts: Linear-algebraic invariants (tr K², Pfaffian), polynomial algebra (biquadratic reduction, discriminant computation) and root-multiplicity classification all interact.  
5. More steps: One must (i) establish the even quartic form, (ii) compute σ, π from traces and Pfaffians, (iii) derive the discriminant, (iv) translate its vanishing into explicit conditions, and (v) analyse each resulting case—considerably lengthier and conceptually denser than the original single-line criterion a₁a₂+a₂a₃−a₁a₃=0.

original_kernel_variant.question:
Let  

             0    c_1   c_2   c_3   
      K =  -c_1   0    c_4   c_5  ,    (c_1,\ldots ,c_6 \in  \mathbb{R}, not all zero),  
           -c_2 -c_4   0    c_6   
           -c_3 -c_5 -c_6   0    

be an arbitrary real 4 \times  4 skew-symmetric matrix.  
For every real parameter x put  

          M(x) = K - x I_4 ,  I_4 = 4 \times  4 identity.

Define the quartic polynomial  

          f(x) := det M(x).  (\star )

(a)  Show that f is an even polynomial of degree 4 and therefore can be written in the bi-quadratic form  

          f(x) = x^4 + \sigma  x^2 + \pi ,                               (1)

     and express the two scalar invariants \sigma  and \pi  solely in terms of the six entries c_1,\ldots ,c_6.

(b)  Give a necessary and sufficient condition, written only with c_1,\ldots ,c_6, for the quartic (\star ) to possess at least one multiple (possibly complex) root.

(c)  Under that condition classify the multiplicities of the roots and write down explicitly every repeated root, distinguishing the three possibilities  

     * x = 0 is a repeated root,  
     * x = \pm  i\sqrt{\sigma  / 2} are repeated roots,  
     * all four roots coincide.  

     Prove in particular that the last alternative can occur only when K = 0 and is therefore ruled out by the standing assumption ``not all c_i are zero''.

(The problem is the 4 \times  4 analogue of the classical 3 \times  3 cubic that involves the Pfaffian and tr K^2.)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
original_kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout we write  

          S := c_1^2 + c_2^2 + c_3^2 + c_4^2 + c_5^2 + c_6^2 \geq  0,   
          P := c_1c_6 - c_2c_5 + c_3c_4  (the Pfaffian of K).

The Pfaffian is real, so P^2 \geq  0.

Step 1.  Reduction to the bi-quadratic form.  
For every 4 \times  4 skew matrix K one has the well-known identity

          det(\lambda  I_4 - K) = \lambda ^4 - \frac{1}{2} tr K^2 \lambda ^2 + pf(K)^2.          (2)

(An elementary derivation uses the block form of K and the Cayley-Hamilton
theorem.)

Replacing \lambda  by x and changing the overall sign (|K - xI| = | xI - K|) gives   

          f(x)=det(K - xI_4)=x^4 - \frac{1}{2} tr K^2 x^2 + pf(K)^2.         (3)

Hence  

          f(x)=x^4+\sigma x^2+\pi                                         (1)

with the invariants  

          \sigma  := -\frac{1}{2} tr K^2,  \pi  := pf(K)^2.                       (4)

Because K is skew, tr K = 0, hence f is even.  
Note that \sigma  \geq  0 and \pi  \geq  0.

Step 2.  \sigma  and \pi  in terms of c_1,\ldots ,c_6.  

2.1  The trace term.  
For a skew matrix, (K^2)_{ii}=\Sigma _j K_{ij}K_{ji}=-\Sigma _j K_{ij}^2.
Therefore  

          tr K^2 = -2 S, so \sigma  = -\frac{1}{2}(-2S) = S.                   (5)

Thus \sigma  is simply the sum of the squares of the six independent entries and
vanishes only when K = 0.

2.2  The Pfaffian.  
A direct expansion gives  

          pf(K)= P = c_1c_6 - c_2c_5 + c_3c_4,                       (6)
          \pi  = P^2.                                              (7)

Step 3.  Necessary and sufficient condition for a multiple root.  

With f(x)=x^4+\sigma x^2+\pi  we have  

          f'(x)=4x^3+2\sigma x = 2x(2x^2+\sigma ).                           (8)

A number x_0 is a multiple root of f  \Leftrightarrow   f(x_0)=0 and f'(x_0)=0.

(i)  x_0 = 0.  Then f'(0)=0 automatically, and 0 is a root \Leftrightarrow  \pi =0.   (9)

(ii) x_0 \neq  0.  Condition f'(x_0)=0 forces 2x_0^2+\sigma =0 \Leftrightarrow  x_0^2=-\sigma /2.
Inserting into f(x_0)=0 yields

        (-\sigma /2)^2 + \sigma (-\sigma /2) + \pi  = \pi  - \sigma ^2/4 = 0.                  (10)

Hence there exists a non-zero multiple root (necessarily imaginary unless \sigma =0)  

          \Leftrightarrow   \sigma ^2 = 4\pi  and \sigma  \neq  0.                               (11)

Combining (9) and (11):

          f has a multiple root \Leftrightarrow   \pi  = 0   or   \sigma ^2 = 4\pi .       (12)

Step 4.  Translation into the entries of K.  
Using (5) and (7),

          \sigma  = S, \pi  = P^2, \sigma ^2 = S^2.  

Condition (12) becomes  

   P\cdot (4P^2 - S^2) = 0.                                          (13)

Equivalently  

   (I)  P = 0, or (II)  S^2 = 4P^2.                              (14)

Because S and |P| are rotation-invariant quantities, (14) is a concise
entry-wise description.

Step 5.  Classification of multiplicities.  

Recall \sigma  = S \geq  0.

* Case (I) only (P = 0, S > 0).  
  Then \pi  = 0, so  

          f(x)=x^2(x^2+S).                                       (15)

  The root x = 0 has multiplicity 2, whereas  

          x = \pm  i\sqrt{S}                                            (16)

  are distinct and simple purely imaginary roots.

* Case (II) only (P \neq  0, S^2=4P^2, hence S>0).  
  Write \sigma =S and set \rho ^2 := S/2>0.  Then \pi  = \sigma ^2/4 = \rho ^4 and  

          f(x)=x^4+\sigma x^2+\sigma ^2/4 = (x^2+\rho ^2)^2.                         (17)

  The two purely imaginary numbers  

          x = \pm  i\rho  = \pm  i\sqrt{\sigma /2}                                 (18)

  are double roots.

* Both (I) and (II) (S=0=\sigma , P=0=\pi ).  
  Here f(x)=x^4, so x=0 would be a quadruple root.  
  However S=0 forces c_1=\ldots =c_6=0 by (5), contradicting the hypothesis
  ``not all c_i are zero''.  Hence this scenario cannot actually occur.

Consequently, for any non-zero 4 \times  4 skew matrix K the only possible
multiplicity patterns are:

   - a double root at 0 and two simple purely imaginary roots (Pfaffian 0);  
   - two conjugate double purely imaginary roots (\sigma ^2 = 4\pi  \neq  0).

--------------------------------------------------------------------

3. Deeper algebra: Detecting repeated roots now requires manipulating quartic discriminants and interpreting them through invariants σ, π, demanding facility with resultants and discriminants rather than the linear observation used in the original cubic.  
4. Multiple interacting concepts: Linear-algebraic invariants (tr K², Pfaffian), polynomial algebra (biquadratic reduction, discriminant computation) and root-multiplicity classification all interact.
